primary_benefits: Opens the chest, throat, and shoulders
Encourages thoracic extension and postural counterbalance
May ease mild tension from rounded shoulders and forward head posture
Supports deeper breathing by expanding the rib cage
instructions: Lie on your back with your legs extended or bent as comfortable.
Slide your hands under your hips with palms down, or keep the arms alongside the body if needed.
Press the forearms and elbows into the floor and lift the chest by arching the upper back.
Gently rest the crown of the head on the floor only if the neck remains comfortable and uncompressed.
Keep the throat soft and the chest open while breathing evenly.
To come out, lower the head and torso slowly and release the hands from beneath the hips.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to broaden the chest and rib cage, and exhale slowly to soften the throat and upper back.
ayurvedic_rationale: Matsyasana is traditionally used to open the heart and throat channels, encouraging freer prana flow through the upper body. The chest-expanding shape can be uplifting for kapha while the gentle backbend helps relieve stiffness and dryness associated with vata.

primary_benefits: Deeply stretches the quadriceps and hip flexors
Opens the chest and front line of the body
Strengthens the back muscles and glutes
Improves spinal extension and mobility
instructions: Lie on your stomach with the forehead on the mat.
Bend one knee and reach back to hold the foot or ankle, then repeat on the other side.
If accessible, draw the heels gently toward the outer hips while keeping the knees comfortable.
Lift the chest slightly and press the pubic bone down to protect the low back.
Keep the shoulders broad and the breath steady as you hold the shape.
Avoid forcing the knees or lower back; reduce the depth if there is strain.
Release slowly by lowering the chest and extending the legs back to the floor.
pranayama_sync: Use slow, even breaths and exhale to soften the thighs and chest without forcing the backbend.
ayurvedic_rationale: This strong opening posture can stimulate sluggish Kapha while building heat and vitality. Because it is intense and expansive, it is best used with care to avoid aggravating Vata in sensitive joints or the lower back.

Thread the hands or reach through between the thighs
Lift the foot off the floor and hold the bent knee behind the thigh or shin to bring it closer to the chest, make sure that the acrum is rooted to the floor

Keep the neck long and gaze slightly forward or down to avoid compressing the neck.
Draw the shoulder blades gently together and down the back.
Hold the posture with smooth breathing and moderate effort.
Exhale to lower the chest back to the floor with control.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to lift the chest and create space through the front body, then exhale to maintain steady support in the back.
ayurvedic_rationale: This mild backbend stimulates circulation and awakens the spine, making it useful for reducing Kapha heaviness and supporting Agni. Its moderate, grounding effort can also steady Vata when performed without strain.

The legs are extended back and the tops of the feet are flat
The palms are flat and the elbows are on the mat, stacked right below the shoulders
On an inhalation, lift the sternum and extend the neck away from shoulders with the elbows, palms and pelvic bone firmly attached to the mat

primary_benefits: Strengthens the back, glutes, and core
Improves balance and coordination through opposite-limb engagement

instructions: Start on hands and knees with wrists under shoulders and knees under hips.
Extend one leg straight back and lift it to hip height or as comfortable.
At the same time, reach the opposite arm forward or keep both hands grounded for stability.
Keep the pelvis level and the core engaged to protect the low back.
Lengthen through the spine and avoid collapsing into the shoulders.
Breathe steadily as you hold, then lower with control.
Repeat on the other side after a brief rest.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to lengthen the lifted arm and leg, and exhale to stabilize the core and lower back.
ayurvedic_rationale: Tiger Pose builds heat, circulation, and coordinated strength, which can help reduce Kapha sluggishness and support healthy movement. The rhythmic cross-body action also helps organize Vata when practiced with steady breath and control.

instructions: Sit with your legs extended straight in front of you and place your hands on the floor slightly behind your hips, fingers pointing toward your feet.
Press firmly through the palms and heels, then lift the hips until the body forms a long diagonal line.
Keep the chest broad and the shoulder blades drawing gently down the back.
Engage the thighs and glutes to keep the pelvis lifted and stable.
Maintain a neutral neck by either looking forward or slightly upward without compressing the throat.
Breathe steadily while holding the pose, then lower the hips with control on an exhale.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to lift and expand the chest, and exhale to maintain steady core engagement and controlled support.
ayurvedic_rationale: This pose builds heat and strength while opening the chest and anterior body, which can help reduce heaviness and lethargy associated with kapha. Its uplifting quality also supports vata by grounding through the legs and stabilizing the shoulders.

pranayama_sync: Inhale to lift and expand the chest, and exhale to soften the shoulders away from the ears.
ayurvedic_rationale: This expansive backbend increases prana and circulation, making it helpful for lethargy and Kapha congestion. Its heating, stimulating quality can aggravate Pitta when the chest and neck are forced or the lumbar spine is compressed.

instructions: Begin from a side plank or three-legged downward-facing dog transition.
Root firmly through the supporting hand and outer foot.

Engage the core and legs to maintain balance and protect the low back.

pranayama_sync: Inhale to lift the chest and open the side body, and exhale to ground through the supporting hand and foot.
ayurvedic_rationale: This expressive backbend can enliven stagnant Kapha and support a sense of lightness and openness. Because it is stimulating and unstable, it may increase Pitta and should be practiced with moderation and clear alignment.

instructions: Begin in a bound side-angle or bound half-bound shape with a stable base.

Breathe evenly, using the exhale to stabilize and the inhale to lengthen.
To exit, bend the lifted knee, lower the leg with control, and release the bind carefully.
pranayama_sync: Keep the breath slow and even, using each exhale to steady the standing leg and each inhale to create length through the spine.
ayurvedic_rationale: This pose is energizing and expansive, useful for mobilizing kapha while building focused, disciplined steadiness. Because it requires strong containment and concentration, it can help channel scattered vata into clear, directed effort.

instructions: Start in a standing balance setup, often from a lunge or half moon variation with one hand on the floor or a block.
Shift weight into the standing leg and lift the back leg until the body is stable.
Place the opposite hand to the floor or block and begin rotating the torso toward the standing-side leg.
Stack the hips as much as possible while keeping the lifted leg active and extended.
Reach the upper arm upward or keep it on the hip if balance is challenging.
Maintain a long spine and steady gaze slightly forward or downward.
Exit slowly by returning the lifted leg to the floor and unwinding the torso with control.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to lengthen through the crown and lifted heel, and exhale to rotate and stabilize without holding the breath.
ayurvedic_rationale: This twisting balance stimulates circulation and clear directional energy, making it useful for reducing kapha stagnation and supporting mental focus. The pose can also refine vata by training steadiness, though it should be approached with calm, measured effort.

ayurvedic_rationale: This pose generates heat and stability while strengthening the side body and core, which can help counter kapha heaviness and vata instability. Because it is effortful and energizing, it is best practiced with steady breath and moderation if pitta is elevated.

instructions: Stand tall with your feet together or hip-width apart.
Shift weight into one foot and ground down through all four corners of that foot.
Place the sole of the other foot on the inner calf or inner thigh, avoiding the knee joint.

Keep the standing hip steady and gently draw the ribs in to support balance.

Release the lifted foot slowly and repeat on the other side.

ayurvedic_rationale: Tree Pose is grounding and centering, making it especially supportive for scattered vata and restless mind states. By building steadiness through the legs and focus through the breath, it also helps mobilize kapha in a balanced way.

ayurvedic_rationale: This strong balancing posture is stimulating and building, making it useful when kapha is sluggish or when clarity and focus are needed. Its stabilizing nature can also help organize scattered vata by training steadiness and control.

With each inhale, lengthen the spine; with each exhale, deepen the fold slightly.
To exit, bend the knees and roll up slowly through the spine.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to lengthen the spine, and exhale to fold a little deeper while keeping the breath smooth and unforced.
ayurvedic_rationale: This forward fold is grounding and cooling, helping to pacify excess vata and pitta through gentle compression and inward attention. It is often used to quiet the mind and support a settling, restorative state after more active practice.

ayurvedic_rationale: Balancing through mindful breath and structural alignment.

primary_benefits: Opens the side body and intercostal muscles
Improves spinal lateral flexibility
Stretches hamstrings, hips, and shoulders
Encourages deeper breathing into the rib cage
instructions: Kneel upright with the right leg extended out to the side and the left knee under the left hip.
Turn the right foot so the toes point forward or slightly up, keeping the left thigh vertical.
Inhale and extend both arms out to the sides at shoulder height.
Exhale and reach the right hand toward the right leg while the left arm arcs overhead.
Lengthen through both sides of the waist without collapsing into the lower back.
Hold the pose with steady breaths, then return to center and repeat on the other side.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to create length through the spine and side body, then exhale as you deepen the lateral bend without strain.
ayurvedic_rationale: Parighasana opens channels of prana through the ribs and flanks, helping release stagnation and improve breath flow. It is especially useful for balancing kapha heaviness and vata tightness while gently stimulating circulation.

instructions: Stand with feet wider than hip-width and turn the toes out slightly.
Bend the knees and lower the hips into a wide squat, keeping the knees tracking over the toes.
Stack the knees roughly over the ankles and keep the pelvis neutral.
Lengthen the spine and lift the chest without overarching the low back.
Bring the arms into cactus or shoulder-height bend, or keep hands at the heart.
Press evenly through all four corners of each foot and hold the posture with steady breath.
To come out, straighten the legs slowly and return to standing.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to lift the chest and exhale to sink the hips a little lower while keeping the breath steady and unstrained.
ayurvedic_rationale: Goddess Pose is grounding and strengthening, often used to increase stable agni without overheating the system. The wide, rooted stance can support kapha by promoting activation while helping vata feel contained and supported.

instructions: From a standing fold or Downward-Facing Dog, step one foot forward between the hands.

Lift the torso and arms overhead or keep hands at the hips for balance.
Square the hips as much as possible and lengthen through the spine.
Hold with steady breath, then step back and repeat on the other side.
pranayama_sync: Inhale to rise and expand through the chest, and exhale to stabilize the legs and core while keeping the breath smooth.
ayurvedic_rationale: This pose creates heat, stability, and forward-moving energy, making it useful for reducing kapha sluggishness and strengthening agni. Its grounding structure can support vata when practiced with measured breath and alignment.

The other leg is extended back with the knee bent and perpendicular to the earth
The back foot is hooked on the inside of the elbow of the back arm
The front elbow is bent upward perpendicular to the earth with the bicep by the ear
The fingers are interlaced to connect the bind behind the body and assist in opening the chest
The gaze is natural and forward
